mcqs.question: Which of the following statements accurately describes the class P in computational complexity theory?

options.text: Problems that can be solved by a non-deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time
Problems that can be solved by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time
Problems for which a given solution can be verified in polynomial time
Problems that require exponential time to solve

mcqs.explanation: Class P (Polynomial time) consists of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine using a polynomial amount of computation time.

mcqs.question: Consider the SAT problem: Given a Boolean formula, determine if there exists an assignment of truth values to variables that makes the formula evaluate to TRUE. In which complexity class does SAT belong?

options.text: P
NP-Complete
Co-NP
PSPACE

mcqs.explanation: SAT was proven to be NP-Complete by the Cook-Levin theorem (1971), meaning it is in NP and every problem in NP reduces to SAT.

mcqs.question: A decision problem L is said to be NP-Complete if: (i) L ∈ NP, and (ii) Every problem in NP is polynomial-time reducible to L. Based on this definition, which of the following is TRUE?

options.text: If we find a polynomial-time algorithm for any NP-Complete problem, then P = NP
All NP-Complete problems are optimization problems
NP-Complete problems can never be solved in exponential time
Every problem in NP-Complete is also in P

mcqs.explanation: If any NP-Complete problem can be solved in polynomial time, then all problems in NP can be solved in polynomial time, proving P = NP.

mcqs.question: Which of the following statements correctly distinguishes between NP-Hard and NP-Complete problems?

options.text: NP-Hard problems are always harder than NP-Complete problems
NP-Hard problems do not necessarily belong to NP, while NP-Complete problems do
NP-Complete problems are a subset of NP-Hard problems
NP-Hard problems can be verified in polynomial time, unlike NP-Complete problems

mcqs.explanation: NP-Hard problems are at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP but may not be in NP (verification may not be possible in polynomial time), whereas NP-Complete problems are in NP.

mcqs.question: Consider the reduction from Vertex Cover to Subset Sum: Given a graph G(V,E) and integer k, construct numbers for each vertex such that Subset Sum instance has a solution if and only if G has a vertex cover of size k. What type of reduction is this?

options.text: Many-one reduction (Karp reduction)
Turing reduction
Truth-table reduction
Log-space reduction

mcqs.explanation: This is a polynomial-time many-one reduction (Karp reduction), transforming instances of one problem to instances of another while preserving yes/no answers.

mcqs.question: The Clique problem is: Given a graph G and integer k, does G contain a clique of size at least k? Which of the following problems can be reduced FROM Clique (using polynomial-time reduction) to prove NP-Completeness?

options.text: Sorting an array
Shortest Path
Vertex Cover
Linear Programming

mcqs.explanation: Vertex Cover is NP-Complete and can be reduced from Clique using the complement graph transformation: G has a clique of size k iff the complement graph has a vertex cover of size |V|-k.

mcqs.question: According to the Cook-Levin theorem (1971), which of the following is the correct interpretation?

options.text: SAT is the first problem proven to be NP-Complete by showing every problem in NP reduces to SAT in polynomial time
The theorem proves that P = NP by constructing a polynomial-time algorithm for SAT
SAT can be solved using a non-deterministic Turing machine in O(n) time
The theorem shows that NP-Hard problems are also in NP

mcqs.explanation: Cook-Levin theorem formally proved SAT is NP-Complete by showing all problems in NP can be reduced to SAT, establishing the foundation for the theory of NP-Completeness.

mcqs.question: Let L1 and L2 be two decision problems. Suppose there exists a polynomial-time computable function f such that for all instances x: x ∈ L1 if and only if f(x) ∈ L2. Also, assume L2 ∈ P. Which of the following conclusions is CORRECT?

options.text: L1 must also be in P because we can solve L1 by reducing to L2
L1 is NP-Complete regardless of L2's complexity
L1 ∈ NP but not necessarily in P since the reduction may not be reversible
L1 must be NP-Hard since L2 is in P

mcqs.explanation: If L2 ∈ P and there is a polynomial-time reduction from L1 to L2, then L1 ∈ P because we can reduce L1 to L2 in polynomial time and solve L2 in polynomial time, giving an overall polynomial-time algorithm.
